chapters.t: Bhakti and Sufi Movements in Medieval India
chapters.text:
25                  Bhakti and Sufi Movements
                      in Medieval India
The Bhakti movement originated in South India between the 6th and 9th centuries CE with the Alvars and Nayanars and later
spread across India between the 12th and 17th centuries. Sufism developed in West Asia much earlier and became firmly established
in India from the 12th century onwards. They promoted social harmony and vernacular literature and challenged religious
orthodoxy, deeply influencing Indian culture.
                                                 UPSC Mains PYQs
      1.     Evaluate the nature of Bhakti Literature and its contribution to Indian culture. (2021)
      2.     The Bhakti movement received a remarkable re-orientation with the advent of Sri
             Chaitanya Mahaprabhu. Discuss. (2018)
      3.     Sufis and medieval mystic saints failed to modify either the religious ideas and practices
             or the outward structure of Hindu / Muslim societies to any appreciable extent.
             Comment. (2014)


1. Bhakti and Sufi Movements in Medieval India
   Dimension                        Bhakti Movement                                         Sufi Movement
 Origins and         y Originated in South India (Tamil Nadu, ca.            y Monastic lineages entered India in the 12th
 Development           7th–8th century), spearheaded by the Alvars             century with the arrival of Khwaja Moinuddin
                       (Vaishnavite) and Nayanars (Shaivite) saints.           Chishti at the dawn of the Delhi Sultanate.
                     y Began spreading from the 12th century and             y Institutionalized through various Sufi orders
                       became widespread during the 15th–16th                  (Silsilas) like Chishti, Suhrawardi, Qadiri, and
                       centuries.                                              Naqshbandi.
                     y Inspired by the Alvars (Vaishnavites) and             y Flourished during the Delhi Sultanate and
                       Nayanars (Shaivites).                                   Mughal rule.
 Key                 y Bhakti (Devotion): Intense love for a personal        y Tasawwuf (Mysticism): Emphasis on divine
 Philosophies          god.                                                    love & unity with God.
                     y Monotheistic tendencies (such as Kabir, Nanak)        y Wahdat-al-Wujud (Unity of Being): Similar
                       & Saguna devotion (such as Meera, Tulsidas).            to Advaita Vedanta.
                     y Social reform: Rejected caste barriers and            y Fana (Self-annihilation in God): Liberation
                       rituals.                                                through spiritual enlightenment.
                     y Salvation through devotion (Bhakti marg).
 Major Saints &      y South India: Alvars (Andal, Nammalvar),               y Chishti Order: Moinuddin Chishti,
 Philosophers          Nayanars (Appar, Sambandar).                            Nizamuddin Auliya, Amir Khusro.
                     y North India: Kabir, Meera Bai, Surdas, Tulsidas,      y Qadiri Order: Abdul Qadir Jilani, Shah
                       Ravidas, Guru Nanak.                                    Badakhshi.
                     y Maharashtra: Namdev, Tukaram, Eknath.                 y Naqshbandi: Khwaja Baqi Billah and Sheikh
                     y Bengal & Odisha: Chaitanya Mahaprabhu,                  Ahmad Sirhindi (who championed Wahdat-al-
                       Jayadeva                                                Shuhud or Unity of Witness to counter monistic
                     y Assam: Srimanta Sankardeva.                             tendencies).
                                                                             y Suhrawardi Order: Bahauddin Zakariya.
 Belief System       Both Saguna (with form) and Nirguna (formless)          Emphasis on Wahdat-al-Wujud (Unity of
                     devotions.                                              Being).
 Path to             Bhakti (love & devotion) over rituals.                  Fana (self-annihilation in divine love) through
 Salvation                                                                   meditation.
 Language of         Regional languages (Tamil, Hindi, Marathi,              Persian, Urdu, Punjabi, Hindavi.
 Expression          Kannada).
 Music & Poetry      Bhajans, Kirtans, Abhangas.                             Qawwali, Ghazals, Kafis.
 Towards             Rejected priestly rituals, idol worship was             Rejected orthodox Islamic formalities, focused
 Rituals             optional.                                               on inner devotion.
 Impact on           Opposed caste discrimination, promoted social           Fostered communal harmony between Hindus
 Society             equality.                                               and Muslims.


2. Causes of Emergence of Bhakti & Sufi Movements
    Causes                           Bhakti Movement                                          Sufi Movement
 Religious         y Rigidity of Brahmanical orthodoxy and                      y Some Sufi thought offered a more
 Factors             dominance of rituals.                                        experiential and inclusive alternative to
                   y Growing discontent with idol worship                         legalist religious practice.
                     (Nirguna Saints)                                           y Demand for an experiential, personal
                   y The exclusivity of Sanskrit texts led to demand for          connection with God, rather than legalistic
                     vernacular spiritual teachings.                              worship.
 Social Factors    y Caste rigidities & untouchability created a need for       y Social inequalities within Muslim society,
                     an inclusive, egalitarian religious movement.                such as distinctions between Arabs, Persians,
                   y Women’s oppression in Brahmanical traditions led to          and Indian converts, created the need for
                     alternative spiritual expressions.                           unity and universal brotherhood.
 Political         y Political transformations under the Delhi Sultanate        y Delhi Sultanate & Mughal rule introduced
 Factors             created new socio-religious interactions, leading to         Persian and Central Asian mysticism to
                     new spiritual explorations.                                  India.
                   y Local rulers like the Vijayanagara Kings supported
                     Bhakti saints.
 Economic          y Growth of urban centres and a rising merchant class        y Flourishing trade routes brought Sufi
 Factors             led to the questioning of rigid caste-based                  influences from Persia, Central Asia, and
                     traditions.                                                  Arabia.
 Cultural &        y Influence of Tamil Alvars & Nayanars who                   y Persian mysticism, Turkish traditions, and
 Philosophical       emphasized personal devotion.                                local Indic elements influenced Sufi beliefs.
 Influences        y Impact of Advaita Vedanta (Shankaracharya) and
                     Saguna Bhakti (Ramanuja, Madhvacharya).

3. Features of Bhakti Movement
        Feature                                 Explanation                                           Example
 Personal Devotion          Emphasized a direct, emotional connection with God,       Kabir’s Nirguna Bhakti (Formless God),
                            rejecting priests as intermediaries.                      Mirabai’s devotion to Krishna.
 Use of Vernacular          Saints wrote in regional languages instead of Sanskrit,   Tulsidas (Awadhi), Surdas (Braj),
 Languages                  making spirituality accessible to all.                    Namdev (Marathi).
 Nirguna Bhakti and         Some saints (Kabir, Nanak) promoted monotheism,           Guru Nanak (Ik Onkar – One God),
 Saguna Bhakti              while others (Chaitanya, Tulsidas) worshiped              Surdas (Krishna Bhakti).
                            personal deities.
 Rejection of Caste         Opposed Brahmanical dominance, promoting social           Ravidas, a leather worker, became a
 System                     equality.                                                 revered saint.
 Space for Women            Women could directly engage in Bhakti without social      Andal, Mirabai, Akka Mahadevi led
                            restrictions.                                             religious discourses.

Nirgun and Sagun Bhakti
The Bhakti movement is characterized by two distinct philosophical traditions of devotion: Nirgun (Nirguna), which focuses on a
formless God, and Sagun (Saguna), which centers on God with specific attributes and forms.

   Dimension                       Nirgun Bhakti                                          Sagun Bhakti
 Concept of           Devotion to a formless (Nirankar),             Devotion to a God with specific forms, names, and
 God                  attribute-less, and absolute Divine.           physical attributes.
 Mode of              Rejects idol worship and external rituals;     Centers on idol/image worship, temple rituals, and the
 Worship              emphasizes inner meditation and spiritual      devotion to incarnations and personal forms of God.
                      realization.
 Philosophical        Monistic or monotheistic, emphasizing the      Focused on a personal Supreme Deity, largely channeled
 Base                 absolute unity of the Supreme Being.           through specific incarnations (Avataras).
 Major Saints         Kabir, Guru Nanak, Dadu Dayal.                 Mirabai, Tulsidas, Chaitanya Mahaprabhu, Surdas,
                                                                     and Andal.


 Examples              y Kabir’s Dohas: Poetry criticizing both         y Tulsidas: Ramcharitmanas, an epic dedicated to the
                         Hindu and Muslim ritualism in favor of a         form of Lord Rama.
                         formless God.                                  y Mirabai: Devotional Bhajans expressing intense love
                       y Guru Nanak: Teachings on Ik Onkar                for Lord Krishna.
                         (One God) and truthful living.
 Primary Deities       None (The Formless Absolute).                    Primarily Vishnu (and his avatars Rama/Krishna) or Shiva.

Re-orientation of the Bhakti Movement by Sri Chaitanya Mahaprabhu
Sri Chaitanya Mahaprabhu (1486–1534 CE) provided a dynamic re-orientation to the Bhakti movement, particularly in Bengal and
Odisha, by shifting the focus toward a highly emotional, collective, and accessible form of Krishna-devotion.

    Dimension                                 Impact                                               Substantiation
 Method of               Shifted focus from solitary meditation to Sankirtan      Popularized chanting the Hare Krishna mantra to
 Worship                 (congregational group singing and dancing).              achieve spiritual ecstasy publicly.
 Institutional           Established Gaudiya Vaishnavism, providing a             Centered theology on the divine love of Radha and
 Base                    structured framework for Krishna-Bhakti.                 Krishna as the supreme reality.
 Social Inclusivity      Radically democratized worship, opening public           Challenged Brahmanical monopoly by prioritizing
                         spiritual spaces to all social strata.                   inner devotion (Bhakti) over birth or rituals.
 Cultural Reach          Broadened accessibility by using vernacular              Enriched regional literature and made complex
                         language, folk melodies, and emotional expression.       theology highly relatable to the masses.
 Theological             Anchored the movement in Saguna Bhakti,                  Prioritized Krishna’s childhood pastimes (Lilas)
 Focus                   emphasizing a personal bond with the divine.             over complex Vedic sacrifices.

4. Features of Sufi Movement
        Feature                                  Explanation                                                Example
 Inner Mysticism             Emphasized spiritual awakening (Ihsan) over external           Fana (self-annihilation in divine love) in
                             religious formalities.                                         Chishti and Naqshbandi orders.
 Concept of Pir              A spiritual master (Pir) guides disciples toward divine        Nizamuddin Auliya, Moinuddin Chishti.
 (Guru)                      realization.
 Brotherhood &               Promoted universal brotherhood, disregarding caste             Baba Farid, Bulleh Shah, Amir Khusro.
 Egalitarianism              and religious distinctions.
 Sama                        Used music, poetry, and Qawwali as forms of divine             Amir Khusro pioneered Qawwali.
 (Music & Dance)             connection.
 Dargah Worship              Reverence for saints' tombs as sites of blessings and          Ajmer Sharif (Moinuddin Chishti),
                             miracles.                                                      Nizamuddin Dargah.

Bhakti and Sufi Philosophy: Similarities and Differences
The Bhakti and Sufi movements (8th–17th centuries) were reformist spiritual traditions that emphasized personal devotion (bhakti)
and mysticism (tasawwuf), significantly influencing India's socio-cultural fabric.

Similarities between Bhakti and Sufi Philosophy
  Dimension                       Common Features                                                Substantiation
 Religious            Both challenged the rigidity of religious                Kabir criticized both Hindu and Muslim rituals; Sufis
 Orthodoxy            orthodoxy and rejected excessive ritualism.              rejected legalistic Islamic formalities in favor of inner
                                                                               devotion.
 Social               Both were egalitarian, opposing caste barriers           Ravidas, a leather worker, became a revered Bhakti
 Outlook              and promoting universal brotherhood and social           saint; Sufis practiced Sulh-e-Kul (peace for all).
                      harmony.
 Path to Divine       Emphasized an emotional, personal connection             Mirabai's intense devotion to Krishna; the Sufi
                      with God based on love rather than priestly              concept of Ihsan (spiritual awakening).
                      intermediaries.


Language of        Both utilized regional/vernacular languages to        Tulsidas wrote in Awadhi; Amir Khusrau used
Masses             make spiritual teachings accessible to the            Hindavi and Persian; Baba Farid wrote in Punjabi.
                   common people.
Music and          Used music and devotional poetry as primary           Bhajans and Kirtans in Bhakti; Qawwalis and
Poetry             forms of divine connection and spiritual ecstasy.     Sama (music/dance) in Sufism.
Spiritual          Centered on the guidance of a spiritual master to     The Guru-Shishya tradition in Bhakti and the
Mentorship         achieve divine realization.                           Pir-Murid relationship in Sufism.

Differences between Bhakti and Sufi Philosophy
  Dimension                       Bhakti Movement                                          Sufi Movement
Origins               Originated in South India (8th century) with       Arrived in India from Persia and Central Asia (12th
                      the Alvars and Nayanars.                           century) with Khwaja Moinuddin Chishti.
Root                  Rooted in Hindu traditions, influenced by the      Rooted in Islamic mysticism, based on the Koran,
Philosophy            Upanishads and Advaita Vedanta.                    Hadis, and Persian mystical traditions.
Belief System         Followed both Saguna (with form/idols) and         Focused on formless mysticism and the concept of
                      Nirguna (formless) devotion.                       Wahdat-al-Wujud (Unity of Being).
Salvation Goal        Aimed for salvation through devotion (Bhakti       Aimed for Fana (self-annihilation in divine love).
                      marg).
Organizational        Lacked a centralized hierarchy; largely centered   Highly organized into structured Silsilas (orders)
Form                  around individual regional saints or sects like    such as the Chishti, Suhrawardi, Qadiri, and
                      the Varkari or Lingayat.                           Naqshbandi.
Language              Vernacular from its genesis; intentionally         Relied heavily on Persian and Arabic for poetry,
                      bypassed elite classical Sanskrit to compose       while creatively adopting local dialects (Hindavi,
                      directly in the spoken languages of the            Punjabi, Sindhi) for popular preaching.
                      immediate population.
Institutional         Temples, community congregations, maths,           Revolved around Dargahs (tombs of saints) and
Sites                 satsangs depending upon tradition.                 Khanqahs (hospices).

5. Bhakti and Sufi Literature
  Aspect                         Bhakti Literature                                          Sufi Literature
Nature           y Devotional poetry focuses on personal devotion          y Mystical poetry emphasizing union with the
                   to a deity.                                               divine (God/Allah).
                 y Example: Kabir's dohas such as “Dukh Mein               y Example: Bulleh Shah’s Kafis – Punjabi poetry
                   Sumiran Sab Kare, Sukh Mein Kare Na Koye.”
Language         y Written in vernacular languages (Tamil, Kannada,        y Predominantly in Persian, Arabic, and later in
                   Hindi, Marathi, etc.).                                    regional languages like Punjabi and Urdu.
                 y Example: Tulsidas wrote Ramcharitmanas in               y Example: Baba Farid's verses in proto-Punjabi
                   Awadhi, making the Ramayana accessible to                 (later compiled in the Guru Granth Sahib)
                   common people.
Forms            y Hymns, kirtans, abhangas, vachanas, and dohas.          y Ghazals, qawwalis, malfuzat (conversations),
                 y Example: Tukaram’s Abhangas in Marathi                    and maktubat (letters).
                   conveyed deep devotion to Vithoba.                      y Example: The Fawa'id-ul-Fu'ad (Malfuzat of
                                                                             Nizamuddin Auliya recorded by Amir Hasan
                                                                             Sijzi).
Themes           y Love for God, surrender, rejection of caste             y Divine love, self-purification, surrender to a Sufi
                   hierarchy, social equality.                               master, and unity of existence.
                 y Example: Mirabai’s bhajans expressed                    y Example: Bulleh Shah’s poetry critiqued
                   unconditional love for Krishna, defying societal          orthodoxy and emphasized inner spiritual
                   norms.                                                    realization.
Influence        y Inspired Bhakti movements in different regions,         y Influenced Sufi practices in India, contributed to
on Society         led to vernacular literature growth, and social           Persian and Urdu poetry
                   reform.                                                 y Example: Amir Khusrau’s Persian and Hindavi
                 y Example: Marathi literature → Varkari Bhakti              poetry enriched Indian linguistic traditions →
                   movement led by Tukaram and Namdev.                       Urdu development.


Contribution of Bhakti Literature to Indian culture
Bhakti literature (6th–17th centuries) served as a transformative force in Indian culture, acting as a bridge between diverse social
strata and civilizations while catalyzing the growth of regional identities and egalitarian values.

      Dimension                        Impact                                             Examples
 Linguistic & Literary     Shifted spiritual discourse from     y Tamil: Tevaram (Nayanmars), Nalayira Divya Prabandham
 Development               elite Sanskrit to vernacular           (Alvars).
                           languages, democratizing             y Marathi: Dnyaneshwari, Tukaram's Abhangas.
                           access to knowledge.                 y Hindi/Awadhi: Tulsidas’s Ramcharitmanas.
                                                                y Kannada: Basavanna's Vachanas (Lingayat).
 Social Reform &           Challenged Brahmanical               y Caste Critique: Ravidas and Kabir rejected untouchability and
 Inclusivity               monopolies and caste                   empty rituals.
                           hierarchies; championed              y Women’s Empowerment: Inclusion of female saints like
                           gender and social equality.            Andal, Mirabai, and Karaikkal Ammaiyar.
 Performing Arts &         Institutionalized music and          y Kirtan: Popularized by Chaitanya Mahaprabhu in Bengal.
 Music                     dance as worship, enriching          y Devotional Music: Purandara Dasa (Carnatic).
                           classical traditions.                y Dance: Transformation of Sattriya (Assam) into classical
                                                                  forms
 Religious                 Fostered interfaith harmony          y Shared Philosophy: Wahdat-al-Wujud mirrored Advaita
 Syncretism                and encouraged interaction             Vedanta
                           between Bhakti and Sufi              y State Policy: Influenced Akbar’s Sulh-i-Kul (universal peace).
                           traditions.
 Philosophical             Evolved rigid Post-Vedanta           y Madhvacharya: Dvaita (dualism) emphasized intense personal
 Growth                    ideas into accessible, devotion-       devotion.
                           centered philosophies.
 Emergence of New          Literature provided the              y Sikhism: Founded by Guru Nanak, rejecting caste for a unified
 Sects                     doctrinal foundation for new,          God.
                           independent religious identities     y Kabirpanth: Community centered on Kabir’s Nirguna
                           centered on monotheism and             (formless) devotion.
                           truthful living

6. Role of Women in Bhakti & Sufi Movements
       Aspect                        Bhakti Movement                                         Sufi Movement
 Women Saints             Actively participated in Bhakti, rejecting        Rare but respected as spiritual figures.
                          patriarchal restrictions.
 Famous Women             Mirabai, Andal, Akka Mahadevi (devotion           Rabia Basri is revered as a Sufi mystic in India.
                          through poetry & singing).
 Women’s                  Gave women a voice against rigid norms;           While fewer in number, Sufi women (like Rabia
 Empowerment              many became spiritual leaders.                    Basri earlier and Bibi Fatima later) were highly
                                                                            respected in mystical traditions
 Social Reforms           Criticised Sati, purdah, and gender               Encouraged spiritual equality but didn't directly
                          discrimination.                                   reform gender norms.

7. Impact of Bhakti & Sufi Movements
The Bhakti and Sufi movements served as powerful catalysts for social and spiritual change in medieval India, fostering a "cultural
mosaic" through their shared emphasis on devotion, inclusivity, and syncretism.

                                                      Bhakti Movement

   Dimension                                   Successes                                               Limitations
 Social Reform        Rejected caste hierarchy and Brahmanical dominance,                 Weakened caste rigidities but failed to
                      expanding spiritual access for Shudras and women.                   dismantle Brahmanical and feudal
                      Examples: Ravidas and Kabir.                                        power structures.


Linguistic &        Shifted religious discourse from Sanskrit to vernaculars,            Egalitarian sects gradually developed
Literary            democratizing knowledge.                                             their own hierarchies and dogmas.
                    Examples: Tulsidas' Ramcharitmanas (Awadhi); Tukaram's
                    Marathi Abhangas.
Arts & Culture      Popularized Kirtan and enriched regional devotional                  Cultural influence remained largely
                    traditions.                                                          devotional, with limited socio-
                    Examples: Chaitanya Mahaprabhu's Sankirtan; Sattriya                 economic transformation.
                    dance.
State &             Offered an ethical critique of political authority and inspired      Many institutions later relied on royal
Political           religious pluralism.                                                 patronage (e.g., Vijayanagara) for
Relations           Example: Warkari Sampraday                                           continuity.
Spiritual &         Advocated direct devotion to God without priestly                    Spiritual egalitarianism rarely
Religious           intermediaries.                                                      translated into structural social change.
                    Example: Mira Bai
Social Welfare      Promoted Seva, humility, compassion, and universal love.             Ethical ideals lacked institutional
& Ethics            Example: Langar                                                      mechanisms for sustained reform.

                                                       Sufi Movement

 Dimension                                  Successes                                               Limitations
Social Reform       Propagated Wahdat-al-Wujud, transcending caste and                Failed to fundamentally alter entrenched
                    religious divisions.                                              caste and class hierarchies.
                    Example: Baba Farid
Linguistic &        Enriched Urdu, Hindavi, and Punjabi by blending                   Literary influence remained largely
Literary            Persian with local dialects.                                      confined to Sufi circles.
                    Examples: Amir Khusrau's Hindavi poetry; Baba Farid's
                    Punjabi verses.
Arts & Music        Developed Qawwali and Ghazal, blending Indian and                 Faced persistent opposition from orthodox
                    Persian traditions.                                               groups.
                    Example: Amir Khusrau pioneered Qawwali.
State & Political   Chishti Silsila preserved moral authority by rejecting            Suhrawardi Silsila accepted state offices
Relations           Inam and state patronage.                                         and wealth, weakening reformist
                    Example: Khwaja Moinuddin Chishti                                 autonomy.
Spiritual &         Fostered interfaith harmony through Wahdat-al-Wujud.              Syncretic traditions faced resistance
Religious           Example: Dara Shikoh                                              from orthodox Ulama (e.g., Aurangzeb's
                                                                                      orthodoxy).
Social Welfare      Established Khanqahs and charitable institutions.                 Welfare remained localized around
& Ethics            Example: Nizamuddin Auliya's service to the poor.                 Khanqahs, with limited structural impact.

8. Major Bhakti and Sufi Saints and Their Teachings
            Saint                                                     Major Teachings
Kabir                          Criticized both Hindu and Muslim orthodoxy; emphasized devotion to Nirguna (formless)
                               God; rejected rituals.
Guru Nanak                     Founded Sikhism; emphasized unity of God, rejection of caste, and importance of truthful
                               living.
Mirabai                        Devotee of Krishna; rejected social norms for divine love; composed bhajans.
Chaitanya Mahaprabhu           Popularized Krishna Bhakti through sankirtan (group singing); started Gaudiya Vaishnavism.
Dnyaneshwar                    Promoted Bhagavad Gita in Marathi (Dnyaneshwari); emphasized devotion over rituals.
Namdev                         Preached unity of all religions; devotional songs in Marathi and Hindi.
Eknath                         Promoted bhakti in Marathi; opposed caste discrimination.
Tukaram                        Varkari sect; composed Abhangas (devotional poems) in praise of Vithoba.
Basavanna                      Founded the Lingayat movement; rejected caste and idol worship; promoted equality.


 Andal                           Only female Alvar poet; composed Tiruppavai, dedicated to Vishnu.
 Khwaja Moinuddin Chishti        Founder of Chishti order in India; preached love, charity, and helping the poor.
 Shaikh Nizamuddin Auliya        Spread Chishti ideals; emphasized renunciation, music (sama), and helping the poor.

9. Contemporary Relevance of Bhakti and Sufi Ideals
The Bhakti and Sufi movements (8th–17th centuries) continue to serve as a "cementing force" for India’s social fabric, providing a
timeless blueprint for inclusivity, pluralism, and ethical governance.

      Dimension                                               Contemporary Relevance
 Social Reform &           The movements' rejection of caste hierarchies and emphasis on human dignity anchor modern social
 Equality                  justice and egalitarian values.
 Interfaith Harmony        Concepts like Sulh-i-Kul (universal peace) and the synthesis of diverse traditions foster interfaith
                           dialogue in a pluralistic society.
 Linguistic &              The saints' use of vernacular languages (Tamil, Marathi, Punjabi) democratized knowledge and
 Regional Identity         remains the foundation of modern regional literatures.
 Arts & Cultural           Devotional forms like Qawwali, Kirtan, and classical dances like Sattriya and Kathak define
 Heritage                  India's global artistic footprint.
                           Example: Joint music festivals, such as annual Jahan-e-Khusrau 2025 Sufi music celebrations.
 Ethics &                  In a material age, the emphasis on inner experience and service (Sewa) provides a spiritual anchor
 Spirituality              and a model for servant leadership.
 Spirit of Public           Defining true spirituality through selfless service to mankind and social welfare.
 Service & Devotion        Example: Widespread community service by modern socio-religious groups (e.g., ISKCON, Sufi
                           khanqahs).
The Bhakti and Sufi movements played a crucial role in shaping India’s socio-religious fabric by promoting egalitarianism and
syncretism. "The Bhakti movement's greatest contribution was that it kept the consciousness of India alive during its most difficult
times." Their legacy continues to inspire India's pluralistic and inclusive traditions today.
pyq: Evaluate the nature of Bhakti Literature and its contribution to Indian culture. (2021)
The Bhakti movement received a remarkable re-orientation with the advent of Sri Chaitanya Mahaprabhu. Discuss. (2018)
Sufis and medieval mystic saints failed to modify either the religious ideas and practices or the outward structure of Hindu / Muslim societies to any appreciable extent. Comment. (2014)

chapters.text:
26                  Buddhism

Buddhism, founded by Gautama Buddha in the 6th century BCE, emphasized Dhamma, non-violence, and liberation from
suffering. It shaped Indian philosophy, art, architecture, and society profoundly.
                                                 UPSC Mains PYQs
      1.     Early Buddhist Stupa-art, while depicting folk motifs and narratives successfully
             expounds Buddhist ideals. Elucidate. (2016)
      2.     Pala period is the most significant phase in the history of Buddhism in India. Enumerate.
             (2020)


1. Origin and Core Tenets of Buddhism

Factors Responsible for the Rise of Buddhism
Buddhism emerged in the 6th century BCE as a transformative Sramanic tradition that provided a spiritual and social alternative to
the prevailing Vedic orthodoxy. Its rise was driven by a synergy of religious, social, and economic factors during India's Second
Urbanization.

      Dimension                           Description                                       Substantiation
 Religious Reaction        A protest against the rigidity of               The Upanishads stressed the atman (inner self) over
                           Brahmanical orthodoxy, complex                  rituals, but Buddhism offered a more accessible,
                           dogmas, and expensive, resource-intensive       structured path for the masses.
                           rituals.
 Social Reform             Buddhism challenged hereditary social           Buddhism opened spaces for Shudras and women;
                           hierarchy by asserting that liberation          for example, the Buddha ordained the cobbler
                           depends upon conduct rather than birth.         Sunita and accepted the courtesan Amrapali.
 Economic Shifts           The rise of a wealthy merchant class            Buddhism was "commerce-friendly," promoting
                           (Setthis) who sought a social status that       trade and frugality. Merchant guilds (Shrenis)
                           matched their economic power, which the         heavily funded the Sangha.
                           Vedic system denied them.
 Agricultural Need         The shift to a settled agrarian economy         The core principle of Ahimsa (non-violence)
                           made animal sacrifices a liability; cattle      protected the livestock essential for wet-paddy
                           were essential for ploughing the fertile        cultivation in the Gangetic plains.
                           Gangetic plains.


Political Patronage       Powerful rulers sought a unifying, liberal       Rulers like Bimbisara and Ajatashatru of Magadha
                          ideology to consolidate their growing            were early patrons. Later, Emperor Ashoka
                          territorial states and counter Brahmanical       transformed it into a state policy through his
                          dominance.                                       Dhamma.
Linguistic                Preaching in vernacular languages broke          The Tripitakas (canonical texts) and Jataka tales
Accessibility             the monopoly of elite Sanskrit, making           were recorded and taught in Pali to accelerate the
                          teachings accessible to the common people.       faith's spread.
Organizational            The establishment of the Sangha provided         Ashoka dispatched state-sponsored missionary
Strength                  a disciplined, dedicated body of monks who       envoys (Dhammadutas) to Sri Lanka, Burma, and
                          acted as missionaries for eight months of        Hellenistic kingdoms.
                          the year.

   Aspect                                    Details                                            Substantiation
Founder           Siddhartha Gautama (563–483 BCE), born in Lumbini,                Became the ‘Buddha’ after Enlightenment
                  Nepal                                                             at Bodh Gaya
Core              y Four Noble Truths                                               Buddha’s First Sermon at Sarnath
Principles        y Eightfold Path (Middle Path)                                    (Dhammacakkappavattana Sutta)
                  y Karma and Rebirth
                  y Nirvana (freedom from suffering)
Eightfold Path


Philosophical     Practical ethics, rational inquiry, emphasis on self-effort       Critique of Vedic orthodoxy and caste
Approach          over rituals or divine intervention                               hierarchy.
Key Concepts      y Anatta (No Soul)                                                Emphasis on universal suffering and its
                  y Anicca (Impermanence)                                           cessation.
                  y Dukkha (Suffering)

2. Buddhist Literature
   Feature                           Description                                             Examples
Tripitakas            Canonical texts of Theravada Buddhism            y Vinaya Pitaka: Monastic rules
(Three Baskets)       written in Pali; divided into three parts.       y Sutta Pitaka: Teachings of Buddha
                                                                       y Abhidhamma Pitaka: Philosophical analysis
Language of           Early texts were in Pali (Theravada),            y Milindapanha in Pali
Texts                 Sanskrit (Mahayana), and later in Tibetan,       y Lalitavistara in Sanskrit
                      Chinese.                                         y Tripitakas translated into Chinese and Tibetan
Jataka Tales          Stories of Buddha’s previous births to           Ajanta paintings, Bharhut and Sanchi reliefs depict
                      impart moral lessons; deeply influenced          Jataka stories like Sibi Jataka, Vessantara Jataka
                      Indian art and literature.
Avadanas              Similar to Jataka tales but not always about     Divyavadana – a popular Mahayana text with legendary
                      Buddha’s past births; focus on karmic            tales
                      effects.
Milindapanha          Dialogue between Indo-Greek king                 Combines narrative with scholastic discussion; popular
(Questions of         Menander (Milinda) and monk Nagasena             Pali text
Milinda)              on Buddhist philosophy and logic.
Mahayana Texts        Emphasis on Bodhisattvas and philosophical       Lalitavistara, Saddharmapundarika (Lotus Sutra),
                      idealism; composed in Sanskrit.                  Prajnaparamita Sutras


 Tantric Texts          Esoteric works incorporating rituals, spells,   Guhyasamaja Tantra, Kalachakra Tantra
 (Vajrayana             and mandalas; flourished in late Buddhism.
 Historical and         Accounts of Buddha’s life and spread of         Ashvaghosha’s Buddhacharita – Sanskrit biography of
 Biographical           Buddhism.                                       Buddha
 Texts                                                                  Mahavamsa – Sri Lankan chronicle
 Commentaries           Later scholastic works interpreting original    Visuddhimagga by Buddhaghosa – influential Theravada
 and Manuals            scriptures.                                     commentary

3. Buddhist Art
         Feature                               Description                                          Examples
 Aniconic Phase                  Early Buddhist art avoided direct                Bodhi Tree (Enlightenment), Wheel (Dharma),
 (5th - 1st century CE)          depiction of Buddha in human form;               Footprints, Stupa; Empty Seat (Meditation)
                                 represented by symbols
 Jataka Narrative Art            Visual storytelling through panels depicting     Bharhut and Sanchi gateways depict stories like
                                 Buddha’s life and Jataka tales.                  the Monkey King, Chaddanta Jataka.
 Jataka Illustrations            Artistic renderings of Buddha’s previous         Ajanta paintings vividly portray Jataka tales
                                 births to teach moral lessons.                   (such as Vessantara, Mahajanaka).
 Transition to Iconic            Buddha depicted in anthropomorphic form          Earliest images in Mathura and Gandhara styles
 Phase                           from 1st century CE onward.                      (Kushan period).
 Mathura School of Art           Indigenous style; robust, idealized human        Red sandstone Buddha statues with urna,
                                 forms with spiritual expressions.                ushnisha, halo, and simple monastic robes.
 Gandhara School of Art          Influenced by Greco-Roman style;                 Wavy hair, Roman toga-like robes, and calm
                                 realistic and Hellenistic features.              expressions. Found in Taxila and Peshawar
                                                                                  region.
 Bodhisattva Images              Especially prominent in Mahayana art;            Bodhisattva Padmapani and Avalokiteshvara
                                 emphasis on compassion and salvation.            murals in Ajanta.
 Mudras (Gestures)               Symbolic hand gestures used in Buddha            Abhaya Mudra (fearlessness), Dharmachakra
                                 images to convey different teachings.            Mudra (turning the wheel of law), Dhyana Mudra.

4. Buddhist Stupa Architecture
The Buddhist stupa transcends architecture, symbolizing the Buddha as the Emperor of the Spiritual Universe.


                                                     Fig. Parts of Sanchi Stupa
     Feature                                    Description                                             Examples
 Anda (Dome)              Hemispherical mound symbolizing the universe or the            Sanchi Stupa (MP) – solid brick and
                          cosmic mountain (Mt. Meru); houses relics.                     stone hemispherical dome
 Harmika                  The square railing on top of the dome represents the           Sanchi – indicating a sacred enclosure.
                          heaven of gods.


 Chhatra                 Triple-tiered parasol above the harmika denoting the            Sanchi Stupa – representing spiritual
 (Umbrella)              Triratna (Buddha, Dhamma, Sangha); Axis Mundi                   sovereignty
                         Concept: cosmic axis connecting heaven and earth.
 Vedika (Railing)        Stone railing enclosing the stupa; demarcates sacred            Bharhut and Sanchi – depict Jataka tales
                         space; often intricately carved.                                and scenes from Buddha’s life.
 Torana                  Monumental gateways in four cardinal directions; richly         Sanchi Toranas – show events like
 (Gateway)               decorated with reliefs.                                         Buddha’s birth, enlightenment, etc.
 Medhi (Terrace)         Circular Platform above base; supports the large                Amaravati Stupa – multiple terraces and
                         hemispherical dome (anda)                                       elaborate decoration.
 Pradakshina             Circumambulatory path around the stupa; promotes                Sanchi and Amaravati
 Patha                   meditative walking worship.
 Material and            Initially made of brick (Mauryan period), later stone;          Ashokan stupas – brick; Sanchi (later
 Evolution               evolved in size, ornamentation, and symbolism.                  phase) – stone with sculptural
                                                                                         enhancements.


Other Buddhist Architecture
     Feature                                  Description                                               Examples
 Chaitya Griha         Rock-cut or structural hall with a stupa at the end for          Karle Chaitya (Maharashtra) – 2nd CE;
 (Prayer Hall)         congregational worship; features barrel-vaulted roof and         elaborately carved pillars
                       colonnaded aisles.
 Viharas               Used as living quarters for monks; initially simple cells,       Ajanta Caves – Cave 1, 2, 16, 17 – serve
 (Monastic             later evolved into complex layouts with halls and cells.         as viharas with exquisite murals and
 Residences)                                                                            structural planning.
 Multi-functional      Later viharas included prayer halls, cells, lecture spaces,      Ellora Caves (Cave 5) – long hall with
 Spaces                and even libraries.                                              benches (possible lecture hall).
 Apse Design           Semi-circular rear end of the hall, often housing the            Bhaja Chaitya (1st century BCE) – early
                       stupa; allows for ritual circumambulation.                       example with horseshoe-shaped apse.
 Wooden                Architectural elements like beams and ribs were carved in        Karle and Bhaja Caves – stone ribs in the
 Imitation in          stone to imitate earlier wooden construction.                    ceiling replicate wooden forms.
 Stone
 Elaborate             Ornamented with intricate carvings, reliefs, and figures         Ajanta and Ellora – Buddha figures,
 Facades and           from Buddhist iconography.                                       Bodhisattvas, floral motifs, mythical
 Sculptures                                                                             beings.
 Integration with      Architectural elements often seamlessly integrate with           Ajanta Cave 1 & 2 – a fusion of painted
 Paintings             painting and sculpture.                                          murals with structural features.
 Adaptation to         Carved into a hillside or rock face, making use of natural       Nasik Caves – strategically cut into rock
 Landscape             contours for design.                                             faces above the river to offer serenity.

5. Factors that led to the Decline of Buddhism in India
The decline of Buddhism in India was not a sudden collapse but a gradual process influenced by internal institutional shifts, religious
competition, and external shocks.

      Factor                             Description                                           Substantiation
 Internal               Fragmentation into splinter groups (Mahayana,         The adoption of Tantric practices in the Vajrayana
 Decadence              Vajrayana) compromised the original, simple           phase alienated the non-monastic(household)
                        teachings of the Buddha.                              followers.
 Linguistic             Shifting from vernacular Pali/Prakrit to elite        Abandoning local dialects alienated Buddhism's
 Alienation             Sanskrit created a severe communication barrier       primary, egalitarian social base.
                        with the masses.


 Religious             The Bhakti movement and Puranic Hinduism                The assimilation of the Buddha into Hinduism as the
 Absorption            dynamically countered and absorbed Buddhist             ninth avatar of Vishnu made a distinct Buddhist
                       philosophy.                                             identity redundant.
 Loss of Royal         State support dried up after Harshavardhana,            The Shungas and Guptas heavily patronized
 Patronage             while rival dynasties actively revived Vedic            Brahmanism, leaving the Sangha financially
                       traditions.                                             vulnerable.
 Social                Traditional support bases among the mercantile          Some historians argue that changing patronage
 Realignment           and artisan classes shifted their religious             patterns and conversions in eastern India contributed
                       allegiances over time.                                  to Buddhism's decline.
 Persecution &         Selective hostility from regional rulers disrupted      Textual traditions such as the Divyavadana accuse
 Hostility             monastic peace and challenged Buddhist                  Pushyamitra Shunga of persecuting Buddhists, but
                       survival.                                               modern historians consider the evidence
                                                                               inconclusive.
 External              Violent foreign incursions physically liquidated        Huna chiefs (Mihirakula) devastated northwestern
 Invasions             monastic centres, dealing terminal blows to the         centres; Bakhtiyar Khalji famously sacked Nalanda
                       religion's infrastructure.                              University (c. 1200 CE).

6. Contemporary Relevance of Buddhist Ideals
Buddhism provides a timeless framework for addressing modern global crises, ranging from mental health to ethical governance and
environmental sustainability. Its core philosophies offer a pragmatic path to social and individual well-being that remains highly
relevant in the 21st century.

     Dimension                    Contemporary Relevance                                          Substantiation
 Mental Health &         The Eightfold Path's focus on Right Mindfulness           Vipassana and Zen meditation are integrated
 Mindfulness             (Samyak Smriti) is widely used to counter digital         into corporate and clinical programs to improve
                         burnout and anxiety.                                      cognitive focus.
 Environmental           The Middle Path (Madhyamika) promotes                     Buddhist monastic rules against cutting trees
 Ethics                  moderation and the interconnectedness of all              align with modern community-based forest
                         beings, prefiguring modern sustainability.                management and India’s Mission LiFE.
 Ethical                 Ashoka’s Dhamma provides a model for servant              Modern anti-corruption bodies like Lokpal
 Governance              leadership, prioritizing the welfare (Praja-Hita)         mirror the oversight roles of ancient Dhamma
                         and happiness of the collective over state power.         Mahamatras.
 Social Justice &        The Buddhist rejection of varna and caste                 Dr. B.R. Ambedkar’s revival of Navayana
 Reform                  hierarchies continues to inspire egalitarian              Buddhism empowered marginalized
                         movements for human dignity.                              communities to fight systemic discrimination.
 Soft Power &            India utilizes its status as the birthplace of Buddhism   The Buddhist Tourism Circuit and Nalanda
 Diplomacy               to anchor its Act East Policy and strengthen              University revival project serve as tools for
                         strategic ties in South and Southeast Asia.               cultural diplomacy.
 Conflict Resolution     Principles of non-violence (Ahimsa) and                   Ashoka's landmark shift from military victory
                         compassion (Karuna) provide a blueprint for               (Bherighosha) to moral conquest
                         peaceful dialogue and restorative justice.                (Dhammaghosha) brands India as a non-
                                                                                   aggressive global power.
 Scientific Temper       The emphasis on rational inquiry and the                  Early Buddhist skepticism toward religious
                         cessation of suffering through evidence-based             dogmas parallels contemporary scientific and
                         practice facilitates a modern spirit of skepticism        logical traditions.
                         and experimentation.
 National Identity       Buddhist iconography forms the bedrock of                 The Sarnath Lion Capital is the National
                         India's state symbols, representing the nation's          Emblem, and the Ashoka Chakra is featured on
                         historical continuity and values.                         the National Flag
Buddhism’s egalitarian ideals, missionary zeal, and cultural patronage significantly influenced Asia. Though it later declined in
India, its legacy endures through art, literature, and global spiritual relevance.
pyq: Early Buddhist Stupa-art, while depicting folk motifs and narratives successfully expounds Buddhist ideals. Elucidate. (2016)
Pala period is the most significant phase in the history of Buddhism in India. Enumerate. (2020)

27                   Jainism

Jainism, emerging in the 6th century BCE, challenged Vedic rituals with its focus on non-violence and asceticism. Led by 24
Tirthankaras, it thrived prominently in India until around the 13th century CE, after which it declined in mainland influence but
persisted in regional strongholds, shaping India’s spiritual and artistic landscape.

1. Core Teachings and Philosophy
    Teaching                          Essence                             Key Concept                    Substantiation
 Pancha                Five vows: Ahimsa (non-violence),                Path to liberation for   Mahavira added Brahmacharya to
 Mahavratas            Satya (truth), Asteya (non-stealing),            monks.                   Parsvanatha’s four.
                       Aparigraha (non-possession),
                       Brahmacharya (celibacy).
 Ratnatraya            Three jewels: Right Belief, Right                Guide to Moksha.         Tattvartha Sutra systematically
                       Knowledge, and Right Action.                                              explains Ratnatraya.
 Anekantavada          Reality has multiple perspectives; truth         Promotes tolerance.      Syadvada’s “maybe” logic.
                       is relative.
 Soul & Moksha         Soul (Jiva) is trapped by Karma;                 Jiva vs. Ajiva           Jīva is inherently pure, eternal, and
                       liberation through penance frees it.             (matter).                omniscient.
 Rejects Vedic         World runs on eternal laws, not a                Self-reliance for        Rejected Vedic rituals,
 authority             supreme deity                                    salvation.               emphasizing individual spiritual
                                                                                                 effort (self-realization).

2. Jain Literature
        Feature                             Description                                             Examples
 Scriptural Division        Divided into canonical (Agamic) and                   Agamas (Svetambara), Shatkhandagama,
                            non-canonical (non-Agamic) texts.                     Kasayapahuda (Digambara).
 Language of                Early texts written in Prakrit dialects               Tattvartha Sutra (Sanskrit), Samayasara (Prakrit),
 Literature                 (especially Ardha-Magadhi), later in Sanskrit,        Jain Puranas in Kannada.
                            Apabhramsha, Tamil, and Kannada.
 Sects and Textual          Svetambara and Digambara sects have                   Svetambara: Acaranga Sutra, Sutrakritanga
 Canon                      different canons due to doctrinal                     Digambara: Shatkhandagama, Kasayapahuda
                            differences.
 Doctrinal Content          Focuses on ethics, cosmology, karma                   Tattvartha Sutra, Samayasara, Ratnakaranda
                            theory, metaphysics, conduct for monks                Sravakachara.
                            and laity, and stories of Tirthankaras.
 Fourfold Division of       Digambaras classify literature based on               Padma Purana (Pratham), Mulachara (Charan),
 Non-Canonical Texts        themes: Pratham, Charan, Karan, and                   Tiloyapannatti (Karan), Samayasara (Dravya)
 (Anuyogas)                 Dravya Anuyoga.
 Commentarial               Jain scholars wrote extensive commentaries            Sarvarthasiddhi by Pujyapada (on Tattvartha
 Tradition                  and digest works to preserve and interpret            Sutra), Niyamsara by Kundakunda
                            original texts.
 Narrative and              Use of legends, parables, and universal               Adi Purana, Uttara Purana, Lilavati Saram
 Didactic Works             history to convey ethical teachings.
 Biographical               Chronicles the lives of Tirthankaras, saints,         Kalpa Sutra (Bhadrabahu), Vasudevahindi,
 Literature                 and illustrious devotees.                             Charitras
 Preservation of            Jain texts contributed to grammar, poetics,           Gommatsara, Tiloyapannatti (cosmology),
 Ancient Knowledge          mathematics, astronomy, and biographies               Parishishtaparvan by Hemachandra.
                            of ancient rulers and saints.


 Moral & Ethical          Emphasis on non-violence, asceticism,              Acaranga Sutra (code of conduct), Ratnakaranda
 Focus                    detachment, and soul purification.                 Sravakachara
 Gender and               Some texts highlight the spiritual equality        Stories of Jain nuns and the role of women in
 Inclusivity              of women, though interpretations vary              Kalpa Sutra.
                          between sects.

3. Jain Art and Architecture
      Feature                         Examples                                              Description
 Emphasis on           Simple meditation cells in Udayagiri-            Austere design, reflecting the ideals of renunciation,
 Simplicity and        Khandagiri Caves, Dilwara Temples                non-violence, and spiritual elevation.
 Symbolism
 Rock-Cut Caves        Udayagiri-Khandagiri Caves (Odisha),             One of the earliest forms, during the Mauryan and
                       Ellora Jain caves (9th–10th CE,                  post-Mauryan periods. Used as monastic dwellings and
                       Maharashtra).                                    prayer halls.
 Stupa Architecture    Mathura Jain Stupa, Kankali Tila Stupa,          Jain stupas are similar to Buddhist ones but often have
                       with railings and narrative sculptures.          images of Tirthankaras and were used to house sacred
                                                                        relics.
 Iconography of        Bahubali statue at Shravanabelagola              Jain art is known for detailed iconography of the 24
 Tirthankaras          (Gommateshwara), sculptures of                   Tirthankaras, mostly depicted in kayotsarga (standing)
                       Rishabhanatha, Parshvanatha with snake           or padmasana (seated) posture.
                       hoods.
 Temple                Deogarh Jain Temple (UP, Gupta period);          Early Jain temples show simple design with emphasis
 Architecture          Jain temples in Aihole, Badami                   on worship halls and sanctum, built under regional
 – Early Phase         (Karnataka).                                     dynasties like Guptas, Chalukyas, and Rashtrakutas.
 Temple                Dilwara Temples , Ranakpur Temple (15th          Later temples show highly ornate carvings, elaborate
 Architecture          CE), Khajuraho Jain temples (10th–12th           mandapas, white marble or sandstone and richly
 – Later Phase         CE).                                             decorated domes and ceilings.
 Towers and Domes      Shikharji Temples, Palitana Temples              Often have multiple shikharas symbolizing ascent to
 (Shikharas)           (Gujarat) with a cluster of shikharas            moksha and feature toranas (gateways) and cloisters.
 Pillared Halls and    Dilwara Temples’ Lotus Ceiling, Ranakpur         Jain temples include beautifully carved pillars with
 Mandapas              Temple’s 1,444 carved pillars, no two            geometric and floral motifs, often with narrative
                       alike.                                           panels and ceiling medallions.
 Symbolism in          Lotus ceiling motifs, Ambika Yakshi              Use of lotus, kalasha, svastika, serpents, and
 Ornamentation         statues, Siddhachakra diagrams in temple         yakshas/yakshis to denote auspiciousness and spiritual
                       walls                                            values.
 Manastambha           Manastambha at Shravanabelagola,                 Erected in front of temples as a symbol of humility,
 (Pillars of Honor)    Moodabidri, Kulpakji Temple                      indicating ego suppression before entering the divine
                                                                        space.
 Regional Influence    Chalukyas (Aihole), Rashtrakutas (Ellora),       Adapted to local styles – Dravidian, Nagara, and
 and Patronage         Solankis (Mount Abu), Hoysalas                   Vesara – and received patronage from multiple
                       (Shravanabelagola), Pallavas                     dynasties across India.
                       (Sittanavasal)
 Pilgrimage            Shatrunjaya (Palitana), Sammed Shikharji,        Temples were often built atop hills symbolizing
 Complexes on Hills    Girnar, Shravanabelagola                         spiritual elevation, forming large sacred complexes.
Jainism, founded on ahimsa, anekantavada, and aparigraha by Mahavira (24th Tirthankara), emphasizes asceticism and karma
theory, significantly influencing Indian ethics, ecology, and non-violent movements.

28                   Foreign Travellers

Foreign travellers visited India across centuries for reasons like trade, religion, diplomacy, and curiosity, leaving behind accounts
that illuminate India's history, culture, and society. Their visit spans from ancient times (circa 300 BCE) to the modern period (up to
the 17th century).
                                                   UPSC Mains PYQs
       1.    Assess the importance of the accounts of the Chinese and Arab travellers in the
             reconstruction of the history of India. (2018)

1. Ancient India Travellers (300 BCE – 700 CE)
Travellers from Greece and China visited during the Mauryan, Gupta, and post-Gupta periods.

Significance of Their Accounts

        Feature                            Description                                            Examples
  Historical                 Provides timelines missing in Indian            Megasthenes’ Indika confirms Chandragupta
  Documentation              sources.                                        Maurya’s reign (302-298 BCE).
  Political Insights         Details administrative systems.                 Hsuan Tsang describes Harsha’s land-grant system
                                                                             (630-645 CE).
  Socio-Cultural             Glimpses into caste and daily life.             Fa Hsien notes the plight of chandalas in Gupta
  Insights                                                                   society (405-411 CE).
  Religious Exchange         Documents the spread of Buddhism.               Hsuan Tsang’s Si-Yu-Ki highlights Nalanda’s
                                                                             prominence.
  Trade and Economy          Highlights trade routes and economic            Ptolemy’s Geography maps Indian ports (130 CE).
                             activities.

Limitations of Their Accounts

       Feature                        Description                                            Examples
 Historical                Relies on secondary sources,             Megasthenes’ claim of "no slavery" contradicts Kautilya’s
 Documentation             leading to inaccuracies.                 Arthashastra.
 Political Insights        Focuses on elites, neglecting            Megasthenes ignores rural Mauryan administration.
                           commoners.
 Socio-Cultural            Misinterpretations due to cultural       Megasthenes lists seven castes, confusing them with
 Insights                  bias.                                    occupations.
 Religious Exchange        Exaggerations lack archaeological        Hsuan Tsang’s claim of 10,000 monks at Nalanda is
                           corroboration.                           unverified.
 Trade and                 Limited depth in economic                Fa Hsien mentions cowries but lacks context on trade decline.
 Economy                   analysis.

2. Medieval India Travellers (700 CE – 1500 CE)
Travellers from Arab, Persian, and European regions visited during the Delhi Sultanate and Vijayanagara periods.

Significance of Their Accounts
       Feature                       Description                                             Examples
 Historical               Chronicles dynasties and political        Ibn Battuta’s Rihla details Muhammad bin Tughluq’s reign
 Documentation            events.                                   (1333-1347 CE).


 Political Insights       Reveals governance structures.          Abdur Razzaq describes Vijayanagara’s fortifications (1442-
                                                                  1445 CE).
 Socio-Cultural           Captures customs and traditions.        Marco Polo notes Southern India’s trade (1292-1294 CE).
 Insights
 Religious                Highlights religious tolerance.         Al-Masudi’s Meadows of Gold praises Gujarat’s rulers (957
 Exchange                                                         CE).
 Trade and                Documents commercial                    Abdur Razzaq praises Calicut’s spice trade (1442 CE).
 Economy                  prosperity.

Limitations of Their Accounts

             Feature                           Description                                          Example
 Historical Documentation          Contains chronology errors.                  Ibn Battuta’s vague "60 days in Multan" lacks
                                                                                precision.
 Political Insights                Elite-centric, ignoring common               Al-Biruni focuses on Mahmud of Ghazni’s court
                                   governance.                                  (1024-1030 CE).
 Socio-Cultural Insights           Cultural prejudice affects objectivity.      Al-Biruni calls Indians "insular."
 Trade and Economy                 Language barriers lead to                    Arab references to "Mount Juran" remain
                                   inaccuracies.                                unidentified.

3. Modern India Travellers (1500 CE – 1700 CE)
European travellers visited during the Mughal and Vijayanagara periods.

Significance of Their Accounts

       Feature                         Description                                             Example
 Historical                 Records political shifts and               Bernier’s Travels covers Aurangzeb’s reign (1658-1669 CE).
 Documentation              transitions.
 Political Insights         Details administrative structures.         Sir Thomas Roe describes Jahangir’s court (1615-1619 CE).
 Socio-Cultural             Offers cross-cultural comparisons.         Nuniz notes Vijayanagara women’s freedom
 Insights                                                              (1535-1537 CE).
 Religious Exchange         Comments on religious practices.           Barbosa details Kerala’s caste system (1500 CE).
 Trade and Economy          Highlights India’s economic wealth.        Tavernier discusses India’s diamond mines (1638-1668 CE).

Limitations of Their Accounts

    Feature                                   Description                                                 Example
 Historical            Focuses on extraordinary events like wars and decline, often        Bernier overemphasizes Mughal decline,
 Documentation         neglecting day-to-day governance and historical continuity.         ignoring administrative stability.
 Political             Shows a Eurocentric bias, misunderstanding Asian political          Sir Thomas Roe major focus on
 Insights              systems, and overlooking regional powers and indigenous             Mughals.
                       governance mechanisms.
 Socio-Cultural        Contains ethnocentric judgments and stereotypes, reducing           Manucci describes Indian customs as
 Insights              complex cultures to simplistic and negative descriptions.           "barbarous" (1653–1708 CE), showing
                                                                                           cultural bias.
 Religious             Oversimplifies India’s diverse religious practices, social          Barbosa reduces complex caste
 Exchange              norms, and caste structures due to limited understanding.           hierarchies to just four, missing regional
                                                                                           and occupational diversity.
 Trade and             Focused heavily on luxury goods, neglecting agrarian                Tavernier highlights diamond trade but
 Economy               economies, rural markets, and internal trade mechanisms.            ignores agriculture, which was the
                                                                                           economic backbone of India.


Travellers by Origin

1. Greek Travellers

    Traveller           Timeline                        Importance of Reconstruction in Indian History
 Megasthenes           302-298 BCE       Establishes Mauryan chronology and administration.
                                         Example: Indika describes Pataliputra’s layout and army of 400,000.
 Deimachus             3rd BCE           Supplements Mauryan history under Bindusara.
                                         Example: Corroborates Megasthenes’ accounts.
 Ptolemy               130 CE            Maps ancient India’s geography and trade.
                                         Example: Geography lists ports and commodities.
Significance: Greek accounts provide the earliest external perspective, crucial for Mauryan history and trade networks.

2. Chinese Travellers

  Traveller         Timeline                          Importance of Reconstruction in Indian History
 Fa Hsien         405-411 CE         Documents Gupta society, Buddhism’s state, and trade hubs like Tamralipti.
                                     Example: Notes ‘chandalas’ and cowries as currency.
 Hsuan            630-645 CE         Details Harsha’s reign, Nalanda’s prominence, and feudalism. Visited the Pallava capital at
 Tsang                               Kanchipuram, and documented the Western Chalukyas of Badami.
                                     Example: Describes Kannauj assembly and land grants.
 I-Tsing          671-695 CE         Arrived via the maritime Spice Route to Tamralipti and spent nearly a decade studying and
                                     translating texts exclusively at Nalanda and Bodh Gaya.
                                     Example: Records precise account of the great Nalanda monastery in Bihar.
Significance: Chinese accounts fill gaps in Buddhist history and socio-political transitions, especially during the Gupta and Harsha
periods.

3. Arab & Persian Travellers

  Traveller           Timeline                          Importance of Reconstruction in Indian History
 Al-Masudi         957 CE               Highlights Gujarat’s religious tolerance and maritime trade.
                                        Example: Meadows of Gold praises Bilahara Rajas.
 Al-Biruni         1024-1030 CE         Provides scientific and cultural insights during Ghaznavid invasions.
                                        Example: Tahqiq-i-Hind covers astronomy and castes.
 Awfi              1220s CE             Records early Sultanate politics and trade.
                                        Example: Details Iltutmish’s relations with Khokhars and Caliph.
 Ibn Battuta       1333-1347 CE         Chronicles Delhi Sultanate’s administration and society.
                                        Example: Rihla details Tughluq’s court and postal system.
 Abdur             1442-1445 CE         Describes Vijayanagara’s grandeur and Calicut’s trade.
 Razzaq                                 Example: Notes 300 harbours and Mahanavmi festival.
Significance: Arab & Persian accounts offer a blend of political, cultural, and economic history, bridging Islamic and Indian traditions.

4. European & Russian Travellers
    Traveller             Timeline                        Importance of Reconstruction in Indian History
 Marco Polo            1292-1294 CE          Documents Southern India’s trade and rulers.
                                             Example: Notes Kakatiya and Pandyan prosperity.
 Niccolò de’           1420-1421 CE          Details Vijayanagara’s social customs.
 Conti                                       Example: Describes caste system and Sati practice.
 Athanasius            1470-1474 CE          Records Bahmani Kingdom’s trade and customs.
 Nikitin                                     Example: Travels notes political conditions.


 Duarte               1500 CE              Chronicles Kerala and Vijayanagara’s society.
 Barbosa                                   Example: Details caste hierarchies.
 Domingo Paes         1520-1522 CE         Describes Vijayanagara’s peak under Krishnadeva Raya.
                                           Example: Notes city’s wealth and military.
 Fernão Nuniz         1535-1537 CE         Highlights Vijayanagara’s culture.
                                           Example: Records women’s role in astrology and wrestling.
 Tavernier            1638-1668 CE         Documents Mughal economy.
                                           Example: Details diamond mines and trade.
 Bernier              1658-1669 CE         Analyzes Mughal decline.
                                           Example: Travels critiques Aurangzeb’s policies.
 Sir Thomas           1615-1619 CE         Offers Mughal court insights.
 Roe                                       Example: Describes Jahangir’s diplomacy.
 Francois             1656–1668 CE         Highlights the absence of a middle class in Mughal India, compares it with
 Bernier                                   Europe’s socio-economic structures.
                                           Example: Notes “no middle state in India ”, only nobles and peasants.

Foreign travellers’ accounts provide invaluable insights into India’s history, culture, and economy, but their biases, inaccuracies,
and selective focus limit their reliability as sole historical sources.
pyq: Assess the importance of the accounts of the Chinese and Arab travellers in the reconstruction of the history of India. (2018)

29                 Indian Philosophy

Indian philosophy, rooted in the Vedas and Upanishads, explores dharma, karma, and moksha through orthodox and heterodox
schools, blending spirituality, logic, and ethics.
                                                UPSC Mains PYQs

Heterodox (Nāstika) Schools

       School                      Core Features                       Key Concepts                      Relevance
 Cārvāka                 y Materialist and atheistic school       y Lokāyata (worldly way)       y Critique of ritualism and

                           (niyati)                                                                and Buddhists.

Role of Indian Philosophy in Monuments and Art

  Philosophy                     Role in Monuments/Art                                          Examples
 Vedanta             Temples reflect Brahman’s unity and cosmic order,        Chola temples (such as Brihadeeswara, 11th
                     inspiring awe and devotion.                              CE) embody Brahman in architecture.
 Buddhism            Stupas and caves symbolize peace, meditation, and        Sanchi Stupa (3rd BCE) mirrors the Four Noble
                     the Buddha’s path.                                       Truths in its dome.
 Jainism             Temples showcase intricate carvings, reflecting          Dilwara Temples (11th-13th CE) depict
                     non-violence and the soul’s purity.                      Anekantavada in art.
 Yoga/               Ritual spaces in temples align with cosmic rhythms,      Thillai Nataraja Temple in Chidambaram, Tamil
 Mimamsa             aiding spiritual practice.                               Nadu, is rich in yoga and cosmic symbolism.
 Epics (Gita)        Sculptures narrate Dharma and Karma, teaching            Khajuraho panels (10th CE) depict Gita’s
                     moral lessons visually.                                  ideals.
From Advaita’s oneness to Charvaka’s materialism, Indian philosophy’s diversity continues to inspire global thought on existence
and liberation.
pyq: Indian philosophy and tradition played a significant role in conceiving and shaping the monuments and their art in India. Discuss. (2020)
